facts: Earth's three main layers are the crust (the thin outer layer we live on), the mantle (a thick layer of hot, slowly flowing solid rock), and the core (the very hot center made of metal); the crust is the thinnest layer, like the skin of an apple.

Volcanic eruptions happen when magma from the mantle rises through cracks in the crust; the lava that cools on the surface adds new rock and slowly builds up mountains over time, showing how volcanism shapes the land.

Earthquakes occur when blocks of crust suddenly slip past each other, releasing energy that shakes the ground; they can cause buildings to collapse and affect people living nearby, making them a geologic event that impacts communities.

Erosion is the wearing away of Earth's surface by water, wind, or ice; over millions of years, it can carve deep canyons and wear down mountains into gentle hills, so the same process that happens slowly can create dramatic landscapes.

Over billions of years, geological processes like uplift (raising land) and erosion (wearing it down) have continuously reshaped Earth's surface, meaning the mountains we see today are not the same ones that existed long ago.
misconceptions: Students believe the mantle is entirely liquid because volcanoes erupt molten rock, not realizing the mantle is mostly solid rock that flows very slowly over long timescales.
Students think erosion happens quickly, like a single flood carving a canyon, when in fact most erosion takes millions or billions of years to significantly alter the landscape.
